subject: Physics
unit: Unit 4: Force, Work, Energy and Power

questions.subject: Physics
questions.unit: Unit 4: Force, Work, Energy and Power
questions.question: A net force of \(80\text{N}\) causes an acceleration of \(4\text{m/s}^2\). What is the mass of the object?
questions.explanation: Newton's second law: \(m = F/a = 80\text{N} / 4\text{m/s}^2 = 20\text{kg}\). Option D multiplies force and acceleration.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 4: Force, Work, Energy and Power
source_trace.content_anchor: A force of 100N accelerates an object by 2 m/s², mass = 50 kg

explanations_tiered.definition: Mass = net force / acceleration from Newton's second law.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Heavier objects need more force to speed up the same amount.
misconceptions: Multiplying force × acceleration (320 kg) is a common error.

publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Remind students to rearrange F=ma correctly.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Calculate mass from force and acceleration

questions.subject: Physics
questions.unit: Unit 4: Force, Work, Energy and Power
questions.question: Why would a car get stuck in mud more easily than on dry pavement?
questions.hint: Friction is needed for wheels to grip and push.
questions.explanation: Mud is slippery and has low friction, so wheels spin without gaining traction. Option A is false; gravity is unchanged. Option D — normal force may even increase slightly.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 4: Force, Work, Energy and Power
source_trace.content_anchor: Why do vehicles easily get stuck in a mud

explanations_tiered.definition: Friction provides the grip for tires to push against the ground.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Mud reduces friction, causing wheels to slip instead of pushing the car forward.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Mud is slippery — your shoes slide, and car tyres slip, so you can't get traction.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Low static friction in mud prevents tyres from exerting a backward force without slipping.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Traction requires static friction. Mud's low \(\mu_s\) means maximum static friction \(f_{s,\text{max}} = \mu_s N\) is too small to accelerate the car.
reasoning_steps: Mud has very low coefficient of friction.
Low friction → wheels spin without forward motion.
misconceptions: Some think mud changes gravity or weight — it does not.

glossary_entry.definition: Grip between tire and road from friction.

flashcard.front: Why cars get stuck in mud?
flashcard.back: Very low friction between tyres and mud.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Why do cars get stuck in mud?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Very low friction between tyres and mud.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Contrast mud with dry pavement (high friction).
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Mud provides very little friction, causing wheels to slip.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.unit: Unit 4: Force, Work, Energy and Power
questions.question: A \(4\text{kg}\) object is placed on a \(12\text{m}\) high platform. What is its gravitational potential energy? (Use \(g = 10\text{m/s}^2\))
questions.explanation: \(E_p = mgh = 4\text{kg} \times 10\text{m/s}^2 \times 12\text{m} = 480\text{J}\). Option A uses only m × h (forgetting g).

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 4: Force, Work, Energy and Power
source_trace.content_anchor: mass 30 kg, height 50 m, g=10 → Ep = 15000 J

explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Energy stored due to height: multiply mass, gravity, and height.
explanations_tiered.beginner: A heavy object high up has lots of stored energy.
explanations_tiered.advanced: \(E_p = mgh = 4 \times 9.8 \times 12 = 470.4\text{J}\) but using given g=10 yields 480 J.
misconceptions: Using only m×h (4×12=48 J) forgets g.

flashcard.front: 4 kg at 12 m, g=10. Potential energy?

questions.subject: Physics
questions.unit: Unit 4: Force, Work, Energy and Power
questions.question: How high must a \(2\text{kg}\) object be lifted to gain \(100\text{J}\) of gravitational potential energy? (Use \(g = 10\text{m/s}^2\))
questions.explanation: \(h = E_p/(mg) = 100\text{J} / (2\text{kg} \times 10\text{m/s}^2) = 100/20 = 5\text{m}\). Option A divides by mg but omits g.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 4: Force, Work, Energy and Power
source_trace.content_anchor: How high should 5 kg object be lifted for 1000 J? h = 20 m

explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Rearrange Ep = mgh to solve for height.
explanations_tiered.beginner: To have 100 J of stored energy, a 2 kg object must be lifted 5 m.
misconceptions: Using h = Ep/m (forgetting g) gives 50 m (option D).

flashcard.front: 2 kg, gain 100 J Ep, g=10. Height?

publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Check students include g in denominator.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.unit: Unit 4: Force, Work, Energy and Power
questions.question: A pump lifts \(150\text{kg}\) of water \(8\text{m}\) in \(6\text{s}\). What is its power output? (Use \(g = 10\text{m/s}^2\))
questions.explanation: \(W = mgh = 150\text{kg} \times 10\text{m/s}^2 \times 8\text{m} = 12000\text{J}\). \(P = W/t = 12000/6 = 2000\text{W}\). Option B is mgh without dividing by time.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 4: Force, Work, Energy and Power

explanations_tiered.definition: Power = work/time; work against gravity = mgh.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: First compute work, then divide by time.
explanations_tiered.beginner: A pump that lifts heavy water fast needs high power.
misconceptions: Using only mgh without dividing by time gives 12000 W (option D).

flashcard.front: 150 kg up 8 m in 6 s, g=10. Power?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: 150 kg up 8 m in 6 s, g=10. Power?
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Check students compute work before dividing by time.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.unit: Unit 4: Force, Work, Energy and Power
questions.question: Can a force act on an object without causing motion?

options.B: Yes, if the force is balanced by other forces
options.C: Only if the object is very heavy
options.D: Only if the force is non-contact

questions.explanation: A force can be balanced by an opposing force (e.g., normal force balancing weight), resulting in zero net force and no motion. Option A is false; forces can cancel.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 4: Force, Work, Energy and Power
source_trace.content_anchor: Can a force always cause motion?

explanations_tiered.definition: A force can be present without motion if it is balanced by another force (net force = 0).
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Unbalanced force causes acceleration; balanced forces result in no change in motion.
explanations_tiered.beginner: A book on a table has gravity pulling down and table pushing up — forces cancel, so no motion.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Balanced forces (e.g., weight and normal force) produce zero net force, so no motion occurs.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Newton's first law: \(\sum \vec{F} = 0 \Rightarrow \vec{a} = 0\), so velocity constant (may be zero).
reasoning_steps: Balanced forces → no net force → no acceleration → may be at rest.
misconceptions: Many think force always causes motion — but balanced forces do not.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Balanced forces → no motion (or constant velocity).

glossary_entry.definition: Forces that cancel each other, resulting in zero net force.

flashcard.back: Yes, if forces are balanced (net force = 0).

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Can a force act without causing motion?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Yes, if forces are balanced (net force = 0).
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Forces that cancel, no net force.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Clarify net force vs individual forces.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Balanced forces (net force zero) do not cause motion.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.unit: Unit 4: Force, Work, Energy and Power
questions.question: A person carries a box horizontally at constant speed. How much work is done on the box?
questions.hint: Work requires force in the direction of displacement.
questions.explanation: The upward force (to hold the box) is perpendicular to horizontal displacement, so work = 0 J. Option D is incorrect because no distance in force direction → zero work regardless.

calculation_steps: Force upward, displacement horizontal → perpendicular → zero work

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 4: Force, Work, Energy and Power
source_trace.content_anchor: carrying a box while walking does not result in work being done

explanations_tiered.definition: Work requires a component of force parallel to displacement.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Lifting force is vertical; displacement is horizontal → perpendicular → zero work.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Carrying a box sideways doesn't 'work' in physics because you're not pushing it forward.
explanations_tiered.advanced: For constant speed, net work = 0. The upward force does zero work because displacement perpendicular to force.
misconceptions: Confusing physiological effort with physics work.

glossary_entry.definition: No work done when force is perpendicular to displacement.

flashcard.front: Carry box horizontally at constant speed. Work?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Carry box horizontally at constant speed. Work?
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Contrast lifting vertically (work done) vs carrying horizontally (no work).
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Determine work from force and displacement directions
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. Force (up) perpendicular to displacement (horizontal) → work = 0 J.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.unit: Unit 4: Force, Work, Energy and Power
questions.question: What is potential energy?

options.B: Stored energy due to position or configuration

questions.hint: Think about energy that is 'waiting' to be released.
questions.explanation: Potential energy is stored energy based on position (gravitational) or configuration (elastic). Option A is kinetic energy, not potential.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 4: Force, Work, Energy and Power
source_trace.content_anchor: What is potential energy? Mention examples.

explanations_tiered.definition: Potential energy is stored energy an object has due to its position or shape.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Examples: a stretched spring (elastic) or a book on a shelf (gravitational).
explanations_tiered.beginner: Potential energy is like a coiled spring — it's waiting to do something.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Potential energy is stored energy based on position (height) or configuration (stretch/compression).
explanations_tiered.advanced: Gravitational potential energy \(U = mgh\); elastic potential energy \(U = \frac{1}{2}kx^2\).

glossary_entry.definition: Stored energy due to position or configuration.

flashcard.back: Stored energy due to position or shape.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Stored energy due to position or configuration.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Stored energy from position or shape.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Give examples: stretched rubber band, book on shelf.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Potential energy is stored due to position or configuration.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.unit: Unit 4: Force, Work, Energy and Power
questions.question: What brings a moving car to a stop when the driver removes their foot from the accelerator?
questions.hint: Opposing forces act on the car.
questions.explanation: Both friction (from brakes, tires, road) and air resistance oppose motion, slowing the car. Inertia (D) keeps it moving, not stopping.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 4: Force, Work, Energy and Power
source_trace.content_anchor: What brings a car moving along a road to stop?

explanations_tiered.definition: Friction and air resistance are dissipative forces that oppose motion.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: When no driving force acts, friction (road/brakes) and air resistance slow the car.
explanations_tiered.beginner: The car stops because the ground rubs against the tyres and air pushes back.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Friction between tyres and road, plus air resistance, provide net opposing force, causing deceleration.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Net force = friction + drag = \(\mu_k mg + \frac{1}{2}C\rho A v^2\), causing negative acceleration.
misconceptions: Some think inertia stops the car — inertia resists stopping, not causes it.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Friction + air resistance stop moving cars.

glossary_entry.definition: Air resistance opposing motion through air.

flashcard.front: What stops a car when accelerator released?

publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Clarify that inertia keeps motion, friction/drag stop it.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer C. Friction and air resistance oppose motion, stopping the car.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.unit: Unit 4: Force, Work, Energy and Power
questions.question: A spacecraft drifts through space with no forces acting on it. What will it do?
questions.hint: Think about Newton's first law in deep space.
questions.explanation: Newton's first law: no net force → constant velocity (in this case, continue drifting forever). Option A would require a force (friction, gravity) which is absent.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 4: Force, Work, Energy and Power
source_trace.content_anchor: a body continues to be in its state of ... uniform motion in a straight line unless acted on by unbalanced force

explanations_tiered.definition: Newton's first law: an object continues at constant velocity unless acted on by a net force.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: In deep space, with no net force, the spacecraft maintains its velocity forever.
explanations_tiered.beginner: If nothing pushes or pulls a spaceship in space, it keeps going the same speed forever.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: No net force means no acceleration, so velocity is constant (including direction).
misconceptions: Objects do not naturally stop in space; stopping requires force.

flashcard.front: Spacecraft with no forces in deep space?
flashcard.back: Moves at constant velocity forever (Newton I).

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Spacecraft in deep space, no forces → motion?
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Emphasise that 'natural state' is constant velocity, not rest.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Apply Newton's first law to space travel
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Newton I: no net force → constant velocity.
